title: Olive Oil on Polyester
timeEstimate: 20–45 minutes; powder + Dawn × 2, cool wash, no fabric softener
stainChemistry: Olive oil on polyester adds the oleophilic + heat-sensitive complications: polyester grabs the oil tight (Dawn × 2 typical) and locks oil under heat (yellows polyester over time when oil is in the fiber). Olive oil is slower to oxidize than vegetable oils so a bit more forgiving on aging, but hot-dried oil yellows polyester permanently either way. Recurring traps: water-first instead of powder-first (spreads oil), hot water (sets oil + yellows polyester), hot dryer (locks residue + permanent yellow), fabric softener (coats fiber, blocks future cleaning).
whyThisWorks: Powder preload pulls bulk oil up. Dawn × 2 strips the oil polyester grabbed onto. Cool wash plus AIR-DRY locks nothing. Skipping the second Dawn pass leaves oil that polymerizes under any subsequent heat as a permanent yellow shadow.
scienceNote: Mechanism (pro-facing): olive oil is mostly oleic acid (monounsaturated triglyceride). Polyester is hydrophobic and oleophilic — extended Dawn dwell needed. Pro uses POG / petroleum ether for oil + NSD.
defaultAssumption: Cooking spatter or salad dressing drip on a polyester shirt or athletic-wear. Performance / specialty / dry-clean-only polyester = pro.

spottingProtocol.technique: Pull bulk oil up before water
spottingProtocol.instruction: Cover the spot with cornstarch / baking soda. Press gently. 20–30 min absorb. Soft-brush off.
Generous Dawn directly. Soft toothbrush 10 min. Polyester is oleophilic — extended dwell. Cool damp-cloth rinse.
Touch with fingertip. Oily feel = repeat Dawn. Polyester typically needs 2 Dawn passes for cooking oil.
Cool machine wash. SKIP fabric softener (coats fiber, blocks future cleaning). AIR-DRY only. NEVER hot dryer until cleared.

steps: 1. Cornstarch / baking soda preload 20–30 min, soft-brush off.
2. Generous Dawn pretreat, cool, 10 min, soft toothbrush.
3. Repeat Dawn pass if oily feel remains (2 passes typical).
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool throughout — heat sets oil into polyester and yellows over time
warnings: Powder BEFORE water — water-first spreads oil
NEVER hot water — sets oil + yellows polyester
NEVER fabric softener — coats fiber, blocks cleaning

homeSolutions.agent: Polyester Loves Oil — Powder First, Dawn Twice
homeSolutions.instruction: Olive oil on polyester is harder than on cotton because polyester grabs the oil tight and locks it under heat. The path: powder preload → Dawn → repeat Dawn → cool wash → AIR-DRY. Three hard rules: powder BEFORE water (water-first spreads oil into a wider ring), NO hot water (sets oil + yellows polyester), and NO hot dryer until cleared (locks residue + yellows fiber).

homeSolutions.agent: Cornstarch or Baking Soda — 20 to 30 Minutes
homeSolutions.instruction: Cover the oil spot with cornstarch or baking soda. Press gently. 20 to 30 minutes. Soft-brush off.
Generous Dawn dish soap directly on the spot. Work in with a soft toothbrush for 10 minutes. Polyester needs longer dwell than cotton because it grabs oil tight. Cool damp-cloth rinse.

homeSolutions.agent: Test for Oily Feel — Repeat If Needed
homeSolutions.instruction: Touch with a clean fingertip. Still oily? Repeat Dawn. Polyester typically needs 2 Dawn passes for cooking oil.

Cool machine wash with normal detergent. SKIP fabric softener — coats fiber and blocks future cleaning. AIR-DRY only. NEVER hot dryer with residual oil — heat polymerizes the oil into polyester and yellows the fiber permanently.

steps: 1. NO hot water. NO hot dryer until cleared. NO fabric softener.
2. Cornstarch / baking soda preload 20–30 min, soft-brush off.
3. Generous Dawn pretreat, cool, 10 min, soft toothbrush.
4. Repeat Dawn pass if oily feel remains (2 passes typical).
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if hot water or hot dryer was used (often permanent yellow shadow). Stop on performance / specialty / dry-clean-only polyester (pro). Stop after 3 home cycles plateau.
warnings: Powder BEFORE water — water-first spreads oil
NEVER hot water — sets oil + yellows polyester
NEVER fabric softener — coats fiber, blocks cleaning
Polyester is oleophilic — 2 Dawn passes typical

neverDo: Never water-first on oil — spreads it
Never hot water on oil + polyester — sets it permanently
fiberSensitivities: Polyester is oleophilic — grabs oil tightly, needs 2 Dawn passes
Polyester yellows under heat over time when oil is present

materialWarnings: Powder before water — water-first spreads oil
No hot water / no hot dryer — polyester yellows under heat with oil
Polyester loves cooking oil — Dawn × 2 typical

escalation.when: Hot water or hot dryer used (often permanent yellow). Performance / specialty polyester. 3 home cycles plateau.
escalation.whatToTell: "Olive oil on [white / dyed / athletic / specialty] polyester, age [fresh / hours / hot-dried suspected], home-treated with [cornstarch preload + Dawn × 2 + cool wash + air-dry]. No hot, no fabric softener. [Residual yellow shadow]."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner with solvent spotting capability for set yellow on polyester

contextQuestions: Cooking spatter, salad dressing, or aged buildup?
White, dyed, athletic, or specialty polyester?
Has fabric softener been used in past washes?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Olive oil's slower oxidation gives a bit more forgiveness on aging vs canola / soybean / corn oil, but polyester's heat-lock makes the difference smaller. Athletic-wear softener buildup is the hidden plateau cause.
